content: [unclear]
Hello, are you ready to get started today?
Yes, I think so.
Great. So today we are going to look at fact families and how they help us multiply and divide.
Okay.
So let me ask you first. Do you know what a fact family is?
Not really.

That is fine. A fact family is a group of related number sentences that all use the same three numbers. For example, 3, 4, and 12. Can you think of a multiplication sentence using those three numbers?
Um, 3 times 4 equals 12?
Exactly right. And can you think of another one?
4 times 3 equals 12?
Very good. Now what about division sentences using those same three numbers?
12 divided by 3 equals... 4?
That is correct. And the last one?
12 divided by 4 equals 3.
Excellent. So you have just made a complete fact family. Those four number sentences all belong together because they use the same three numbers. Does that make sense?
Yes, I think so.
Let us try another one. I will give you the three numbers and you give me all four number sentences. The numbers are 5, 7, and 35.
[unclear] Um, 5 times 7 equals 35.
Good, keep going.
7 times 5 equals 35. And then 35 divided by 5 equals 7.
Very good. One more.
35 divided by 7 equals 5.
Fantastic. You have got all four. Now can you see why knowing your times tables helps with division as well?
Because they use the same numbers?

Exactly right. When you know that 5 times 7 is 35, you automatically know that 35 divided by 7 is 5. It is all connected. Let us try a slightly harder one now. The numbers are 6, 8, and 48. Can you give me all four?
6 times 8 equals 48, and 8 times 6 equals 48. Then 48 divided by 6 equals 8, and 48 divided by 8 equals 6.
Perfect, very well done. Now I want you to look at this question here. If 9 times 4 equals 36, what is 36 divided by 9?
Correct. And what is 36 divided by 4?
Well done. Now let us think about scaling. If 5 times 3 equals 15, what do you think 5 times 30 equals?
That is exactly right. Can you explain how you figured that out?
Because 30 is 10 times bigger than 3, so the answer is 10 times bigger.
Excellent explanation. So when we multiply one of the numbers by 10, the answer also gets 10 times bigger. What about 50 times 3?
That would also be 150.
Correct again. And 50 times 30?
[unclear] Would it be... 1500?
Very good. Now let us look at what happens with division. If 15 divided by 3 equals 5, what is 150 divided by 3?
Right. And 150 divided by 30?
That is correct. So the extra zero on the bottom cancels out the extra zero on the top. Let us try a problem now. Can you read this question out please?
If 7 times 8 equals 56, use this fact to work out 560 divided by 8.
Good. So what is 560 divided by 8?
How did you work that out?
Because 56 divided by 8 is 7, but there is an extra zero, so it is 70.
Perfect. Let us keep practising. Here is another one. 4 times 9 equals 36. What is 360 divided by 4?
Well done. Now let me show you a triangle diagram that helps with fact families. You put the largest number at the top and the two factors at the bottom.
Okay.
So if I put 24 at the top, and then 3 and 8 at the bottom, what multiplication sentences can we make?
3 times 8 equals 24, and 8 times 3 equals 24.
And the division sentences?
24 divided by 3 equals 8, and 24 divided by 8 equals 3.
Brilliant. Let me give you a challenge question. I am thinking of a number. When I multiply it by 7, I get 63. What is my number?
Correct. How did you figure that out?
Because 9 times 7 is 63 from the 9 times table.
Excellent. Now here is a harder one. When I multiply my number by 6, I get 480. What is my number?
[unclear] Um...
Think about it this way. Do you know 8 times 6?
Yes, it is 48.
So what is 80 times 6?
Oh, 480. So the number is 80.
Very well done. Let us now try some questions where there are zeros involved. If 6 times 4 equals 24, what is 240 divided by 6?
Good. And what is 2400 divided by 6?
Excellent. Can you explain the pattern?
Every extra zero you add on top, the answer gets another zero too.
That is a very good way of putting it. Let me give you a mixed set of questions now. Take your time and let me know if you need help.
Okay.
[unclear]
I got 7 for the first one.
Let us check. 7 times 9 is 63, yes that is right. How about the second one?
I got 500.
Can you explain how?
Because 5 times 6 is 30, so 3000 divided by 6 is 500.
Very well done. Now let us look at this question together. [unclear]
Okay. So 8 times 7 equals 56.
And what does that tell us about 56 divided by 7?
It equals 8.
Good. Now use that to help with 5600 divided by 7.
Correct. You are doing really well today. Shall we move on to a challenge?
Yes please.
Here is a real-life problem. A school has 360 pencils. They are shared equally between 9 classes. How many pencils does each class get?
That would be 40.
How did you work that out?
I know 4 times 9 is 36, so 40 times 9 is 360.
Excellent thinking. Let me give you another one. A baker makes 480 rolls. He packs them into bags of 6. How many bags does he need?
[unclear] Um, 80.
Very good. And another. A number is multiplied by 8 and the answer is 720. What is the number?
How do you know?
Because 9 times 8 is 72, so 90 times 8 is 720.
That is great reasoning. One more challenge. Complete the fact family for these numbers: 70, 8, 560.
70 times 8 equals 560. 8 times 70 equals 560. 560 divided by 8 equals 70. And 560 divided by 70 equals 8.
Perfect, all four number sentences correct. You have really understood fact families today.
Thank you.
Do you have any questions before we finish?
No, I think I understand now.
Brilliant work today. We will carry on with more multiplication and division practice next time. See you then.
Bye.
